summary: DHH shares his recent discovery of mechanical keyboards and the satisfying 'thock' sound they produce. After years of contentment with Apple's Magic Keyboard, he fell into the mechanical keyboard rabbit hole and found unexpected daily joy in the typing experience. He celebrates the incredible variety and affordability in the mechanical keyboard market as a triumph of capitalism and competition. He recommends two specific boards — the Varmilo Milo 75 and Lofree Flow 84 — as entry points for anyone curious about upgrading their typing experience.
key_points: Mechanical keyboards don't solve problems — they add sensory delight to the mundane act of typing, which is its own reward
The mechanical keyboard market showcases capitalism at its best: hundreds of manufacturers competing on niche details at increasingly affordable prices
The hobby has evolved from requiring DIY assembly to offering excellent prebuilt options, making it far more accessible
DHH had false starts with Keychron boards before finding keyboards he truly enjoyed, suggesting first impressions of the hobby can be misleading
money_quotes: Mechanical keyboards don't solve any problems. In fact, if anything, they bring quite a few with them!
Taking greater joy from such a mundane activity as typing is one of those rare daily treats that just makes life a little better, a little more interesting.
It's also a benign rabbit hole of lingo, knowledge, aesthetics, and preference. Kinda like mechanical watches, but mercifully more affordable.
The sheer variety and depth of this market would make Adam Smith blush.
I absolutely love leveling up on a new domain like this!
Both look amazing, feel amazing, and make a mockery of the $100 Apple Magic Keyboard that I thought was all I ever needed for so long.
key_insight: The best tools aren't always about solving problems — sometimes they're about adding unexpected delight to the daily grind of work you're already doing.
